introduction.title: Vietnamese Exclamations & Particles
content: Vietnamese is full of small particles that color speech with emotion — surprise, confirmation, sympathy, doubt. Without them, sentences sound flat and bookish.
Some are exclamation words used alone ('Ôi!', 'Trời ơi!') and others are sentence-final particles ('à', 'nhỉ', 'đấy') that change the tone of a statement.
Mastering a handful of these is one of the fastest ways to sound natural in Vietnamese conversation.

sections.title: Interjections (Standalone Exclamations)
sections.description: Words you can shout on their own to express emotion

notes: General-purpose 'oh!' for surprise, realization, or sympathy.
Often combined: 'Ôi trời!' (Oh heavens!).
Mild and very common.
Literally 'sky/heaven hey!' — the most common strong exclamation.
Used for shock, frustration, amazement — context decides which.
Equivalent to English 'OMG!' or 'oh my goodness!'.
Used for sudden physical pain or small accidents.
Like English 'ouch!' or 'oops!'.
Short and reactive.
Expresses disgust or revulsion.
Used for bad smells, gross sights, or distaste.
More common in northern speech.
Expresses admiration or pleasant surprise.
Softer and more appreciative than 'trời ơi'.
Often followed by a compliment.

sections.title: Intensity Particles
sections.description: Words that turn a statement into an exclamation — the backbone of Vietnamese exclamations

notes: THE most common exclamation particle in Vietnamese.
Placed AFTER the adjective or verb: 'đẹp quá!' (so beautiful!).
Works for both positive and negative: 'ngon quá!' (so tasty!) vs 'đắt quá!' (too expensive!).
Placed AFTER the adjective, like 'quá'.
Implies surprise or realization — 'I didn't expect it to be this much'.
Softer than 'quá'.
Literally 'true' + 'is' — placed BEFORE the adjective.
More formal and literary than 'quá'.
Position: ~~tuyệt vời thật là~~ __thật là tuyệt vời__ (goes before, not after).

examples.notes: Implies: I didn't realize you were this good

examples.vietnamese: Cảnh này thật là tuyệt vời!

sections.title: Sentence-Final Particles
sections.description: Tiny words at the end of sentences that change tone and feeling

notes: Turns a statement into a soft question or expression of mild surprise.
Very gentle — used in everyday conversation.
Seeks agreement from the listener.
Friendly and inviting — assumes the listener agrees.
Adds emphasis or 'pointing out' feeling.
Slightly informal — common in northern speech.
Southern equivalent: 'đó'.
Adds a sense of 'just' or 'enough already'.
Can also mean 'stop it' as a standalone.
